item.title: Canadian Mortgages are insane — 67 replies — 154.5/hr
item.content:encoded:
Canadian Mortgages are insane

Open thread • Replies: 67 • Updated: 2026-06-20 09:10 UTC

OP

Anyone that bought homes over the last 10 years has no concept of the magnitude of the level of debt they have.

Imagine 400k to 900k mortgages on cookie cutter homes with no space between them. This is just fine in the mind of the average leaf.

Let me be clear, they are never going to pay it off. Never. They think because everyone else has a big ass mortgage that its fine. It took me 17 years just to pay down my 228k mortgage to 68k where it is now. How the fuck do these people think at 38 with a 500k mortgage they can pay it down by the time they retire? LMAO.

Fucking totally disconnected from reality. Millions of us.

Early replies (first 3)

62382768
im sick of this being a thing, just fucking crash already. I have to deal with crashes 100 times a year as a crypto cuck but everyone else in the world with their houses and their gay stocks is just driving off into the sunset. make it make sense nigger

62382788
>>62382765
their retirement plan is your pic

62382793
>>62382788
I find this pick really depressing because it reminds me of my parents. They are just in another fucking world. You cannot convince them that anything is wrong.

62389493
>>62389479
This too>>62389460
Canada has what it takes be a super power now.

62389498
>>62389485
> Theres a reason new builds are stopping/slowing down
This. One reason why no one wants to make starter homes.

The goal is to pump homes prices so high that boomers bagholding their 2 millions dollars homes are forced to sell due to taxes and then megacorps can sweep in and knock down whole neighborhoods to build 5on1 jeets breeding nests

62389551

62389564
>>62382765
Mortgages usually have a payment schedule that results in them being paid off over 30 years. You are a retard if you pay them faster than that with a good rate. I work with commercial mortgages professionally, borrower side, and even as a company you are retarded to not simply pay the normal amortization payment. Dave Ramsy is a fucking idiot and you are too.

62389574
>>62389564
ur not as smart as you think you are bc you dont have a home paid off and 6 figures liquid. no reply is going to make me believe u know how to manage large sums of cash

62389582
>>62389574

62389587
>>62389564

62389591
>>62389582

62389595

62389597
>>62389479

62389603

62389612
>>62382765
Don't you guys only have variable rate mortgages and no ability to deduct mortgage interest from your taxable income? Canada real estate is fucked.

62389627
Canadian boomers are by far, the absolute scum of the Earth.

62389630
>>62389564
>I work with commercial mortgages professionally
Oh look, a guy who sells debt for a living thinks you should be in more debt. Shocker. Enjoy bankruptcy as the commercial real estate market craters from remote work.

Nothing wrong with paying down debt. Interest is a guaranteed loss. Investing is a speculative gain. There is a solid argument that getting rid of any amount of guaranteed loss is better than a non-guaranteed gain.

62389661
>>62389630
I don't own any office with mortgages anymore so not really a risk. That already all died in the 2010s.

62389703
>>62389498
There is so much incumbency protection. The boomers own most of the real estate and most of the voting power, so they can get the politicians to just make it harder and harder to create more competing housing, which drives up their home values. The whole west coast of the US has similar problems.

62389713
>>62389703
agree with u about the Mortgage arsehole but you are just wrong here mate

62389716
>>62389595
WTF are you even talking about.

62389727
>>62389333
Of course we do, but the longest terms are 5 years and then they get renegotiated. This is great when rates are dropping, but now they are trending up. I did very well actually with my rates going down every time I renewed, except the last time, but with my principal so low now, it doesn't matter.

62389743
>>62389493
A superpower requires a large unified homogeneous population to project power. We (Canada) have done the opposite. We unironically had more power 20 years ago.

62389746
>>62389743
the Trudeau's basically sold Canada but i wouldnt be surprised if it has a ded xD cat bounce

62389750

62389752
>>62389716
I understand why Canadians kill themselves considering they don't have fixed rate mortgages.

62389952
>>62389601
'high' taxation and 'over'-regulation are unironically the stepping stones to abundant and fun and healthy and joyful to live in societies. for example a 34% tax on synthetic/natural combination clothes, like 80% cotton / 20% poly blends, these textiles are notoriously hard to recycle and apparently just end up in landfills, the tax can fund incentives like grants and tax breaks to companies developing recycling processes that can handle stuff like that. Weighing garbage at pickup with scales in the truck and tracking how much each household puts out and charging them fees based on that, rather than a flat rate. Stuff to incentivize treating the environment better. Canada already has relatively lots of regulation on resource extraction. Americans wouldn't really comply with such nudges but since Canadians are more docile in this sense, it leads to better outcomes. The company developing graphene production from fly ash from incinerated trash is headquartered in Ontario, and graphene added to concrete can make it more resilient and able to support higher loads, something like that would free up the forests from clearcutting at such scales, allow them to regenerate, and the knock-on effect would be less ugly paper-thin houses. etc

BC is 2x the size of Texas, but suburban developments are bug-like hives

62390319
>>62382765
I have 2 properties, no debt and a job I love. There is no stress in my life, no timing markets, just peace, and doing what gives my life meaning.

item.title: How the hell is the US still outpacing Europe for economic growth after everything that's happened? — 29 replies — 116.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
How the hell is the US still outpacing Europe for economic growth after everything that's happened?

Open thread • Replies: 29 • Updated: 2026-06-20 09:34 UTC

OP

How the hell is the US still outpacing Europe for economic growth after everything that's happened?

Early replies (first 3)

62387242
>>62387239
Who cares

62387246
>>62387239
Wendeez nuts hit your chin

62387248
>>62387239
Because GDP has always been a meme.

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62387300
Too busy raping kids

62387318
>>62387239
Federal job cuts and govt spending in key sectors, AI efficiency gains, imperialistic tariffs, deregulation. Basic economic theory. Everything the media said was bad was actually good for GDP growth

62387322
>jump up and down on the street in front of wumbo sized Wendy, arms flailing desperately over your head, making a complete fool of yourself
>her huge eyeballs shift down to meet your diminutive form
>WENDY! WENDY! HEY WENDYYYY!
>the sky darkens as her huge smug face takes up your whole vision
>I-I-I love Wendys! I get a Breakfast Baconator every morning on the way into work! And I doordash the spicy nuggets all the time, twenty piece! W-with a Frostie, those are the best!
>she stares you into the ground for a few seconds before laughing like a thunderclap above your head
>her hand moves to one of her huge, bus sized shoes and begins slipping it off
>"well, how about a free ride for my favorite customer~?"

62387388
europe is a bureaucratic hellhole, nothing happens without a massive amount of paper work, it's a nightmare for entrepreneurs, no wonder they can't into ai, all their researchers are in america

62387389
>>62387388
/thread

You can't even shoot someone killing you in Europe. Pathetic nations all around.

62387404
>>62387239
because 2/3 of their GDP dollar value comes from AI or AI related industry, the whole shebang is just make belief and it works as long as the rest of the world agrees to play along, which it currently does. But oh boy, the second the facade drops and everyone can see the corpse they're parading around as a savior of the economy..

62387420
>>62387388
My problem (as an entrepreneur) is that to get soft funding, you have to precisely fit a mould. For example, there are lots of small check options, but only if you're associated with this university, or that university. Or only if you're a woman. Or only if you're working on climateshit. As for hard funding, investors don't want to take risk. I saw unironically a French investor who claim they only invest in pre-seed, but only if you have 100k+ ARR and their max ticket size is 250k and they exclusively lead rounds (meaning max round size is 500k). How the fuck is this pre-seed then, and why the fuck would anyone take 250k if they are already at 100k ARR? That's mostly an exception, usually the problem is they can't fucking decide and then cry about it.
But American investors are far worse, they don't want to invest in anything that's not AI. "Sorry but we only do preseed if you have clients. Oh you have clients uh... b-but did you talk to a big player ye- you did and you have a written statement that they'll buy at a high price if you can implement feature X? uh (damn, how can I say no...) oh uh w-we only invest if you have at least 100 b2b clients all paying 50k+ [these are series B stats btw]"
Worst part is Americans and their fans keep claiming it's the opposite, that American investors try to find a reason to accept while Europeans try finding a reason to reject. I want to punch Paul Graham in the face everytime he opens his lying whore mouth about America, SF investors, or YC, everything he says is the opposite of reality. But that also applies to other Americans.

62387614
>>62387239
Why wouldn't it be?

* The US has far cheaper energy and chemical feedstocks.
* The US benefits from economies of agglomeration and network effects in important growth sectors such as information tech.
* The US isn't letting itself get bossed around by the US like the EU is, which makes the US look like a better place to invest.

62387618
Is it not the most obvious answer in the world, I mean really if you actually sit down and think about it for 2 milliseconds

62387621
>>62387239
I am european and I work like 4 hours a day and I spend my days strolling, drinking with friends and playing vidya.
That's how
we're europemaxxing

62388089
>>62387239
wtf do you mean "everything that's happened"?
you mean europe fostering an underclass of people who don't and wont work? you mean europe actively building ghettoes and cultivating race riots? you mean europe taxing and regulating all innovation away? you mean europe dismantling its power plants to chase fantasies?
europe crippled itself economically and there is no financial reason to ever start a company here over the us so most of the great startups leave

62388103
>>62387621
Sounds peaceful
Good luck with the foreign invasion

62388287
>>62388089
What about the tariffs?

62388362
>>62387239
Both the US and Europe are in decline, but both have very far to fall, in spite of acceleration. The US has always outperformed Europe economically, while Europe has mogged the US in history, cultural diversity, and art.

62388366
>>62388362
And science and tech. The pipeline is often that europe invents some pivotal tech but fails to monetize it because the infrastructure for this is dogshit in europe, so either some us company copies it and wins the market, the european company moves to the us, or a us company purchases the european company and markets the tech through the main office.

62388374
>>62388366
I agree but it's also the pipeline of the past. I'd also add that the US university research system is pretty good at science and tech discovery as well. But lately, a lot of the monetization and scaling manufacturing for non software tech discovered or invented in the US or Europe has come from China.

62388389
>>62388374
Yes, it's true that modern US research unis are pretty good. However they are much better at publicizing their work than they are in actually doing novel work, in relative terms, compared to their EU peers. For example, pretty much all modern biology works on purely EU tech. But you will only read about what the US has contributed (or thinks it might contribute some day) if you look up information about biology advances.

62388394
>>62388389
I don't know as much about biology discovery pipelines admittedly, but I could see that and it makes sense.

62389689
>>62387318
> muh basic economic theory while ignoring consumer debt and wage stagnation

gdp numbers don't put food on the table for most people

62389710
>>62387388
> muh paperwork

europe's been coasting on welfare and red tape for decades, now they're shocked the brain drain is real.

62390217
>>62389710
Europe has been braindraining the US for a couple years now. The only reason it had gone the other way until now has been that the US is flooded with free money going around (ironic then that you blame welfare!) for business activities and salaries. But this has largely equalized for salaries (not quite there yet but you could say it's there if you add the work conditions and life conditions in the US have greatly deteriorated and many are no longer willing to get the salary difference to put up with it). It hasn't yet equalized for funding though, but it's become just hard enough to raise a business instead of a scam in the US that people who want to actually make real businesses don't feel the need to go to the US anymore (more like, US investors no longer invite them in, as it were).

62390244
>>62388089
Man you really drank the russian propaganda.

62390247
>>62387239
Is it really though? Or is it all just stock market inflation money bubble?

62390270
>>62390247
>statistics aren't real
>>62390244
>russia made them
>europe is actually doing really well economically just not by any measurable metric

62390282
>>62390270
gdp is well accepted to not be real in any way, yes.

62390345
>>62387239
>took over the empire
>work harder and more
>have more resources, more land
Truly a mystery.

item.title: UNDER $10K NET WORTH THREAD #1 — 82 replies — 69.1/hr
item.content:encoded:
UNDER $10K NET WORTH THREAD #1

Open thread • Replies: 82 • Updated: 2026-06-20 08:24 UTC

OP

Brothers,

SpaceX has begun to rise. Its success or fall is of little importance to us in the short term.

In the meanwhile we must devise ways to gain a spot at the table with our kin in the six figure club.

I for one propose we spend our time learning a subject. Knowledge is the greatest wealth of all.

However, I would be remiss to not tell the truth that I haven't a clue what we should study.

So instead I suggest we create an eggregore for these threads. Others made monero-chan, bobo and sminem

I am thinking we create one based on a girl who would be willing to believe in us while we are poor and brave the pain throughout having to witness our more disappointing moments of action. Never losing faith in us, she is always there. Yes. This is how we shall spend our time.

At any rate, that is my suggestion for tonight's fun. I have to work in the morning so let's not get too rowdy. Take it or leave it. If you will take it, then be sure to double it and pass it on to the next anon.

Let us commence the general!

Come one, come all!

>>62373752

item.title: >boss put me on a PIP 2 months ago >been trying to find jobs a few months before that cuz I realized there was no pleasi… — 78 replies — 65.3/hr
item.content:encoded:
>boss put me on a PIP 2 months ago >been trying to find jobs a few months before that cuz I realized there was no pleasi…

Open thread • Replies: 78 • Updated: 2026-06-20 08:24 UTC

OP

>boss put me on a PIP 2 months ago
>been trying to find jobs a few months before that cuz I realized there was no pleasing him
>haven't found any jobs, its unreal how bad the market is, have this bad feeling its never going to get better
>PIP ends on Tuesday
I am an engineer. I started off as a tech and worked my ass off getting bachelors full time to escape manual labor. Now im gonna be that goy with a masters degree who took a 500 percent pay cut to do repetitive ligament destroying work. I honestly feel like god did this beacuse its too much bad things happening at once that are culminating into my total demise.

Early replies (first 3)

62388691
>>62388676
>500 percent pay cut to do repetitive ligament destroying work
Trades pay more than ever, especially vs pajeet-saturated office work. Skill issue

62388694
>>62388676
My entire life has been nothing but fighting tooth and nail, working myself 1000x harder than anyone anywhere ever for 0.001% of what anyone else gets for free. I have felt that god is purposely shitting on me pretty much my whole life. It doesn't get better, it only ever gets worse. If anything happens to me that seem to be a good thing, I have 6 months max to enjoy it because that'll be taken from me and then I'll be beaten up and crushed down for added effect. It's a real comedy out there.

So don't worry bro, you have a lot of time ahead of you to suffer far more. If there really is a god, he must be the most perfectly evil of entities.

62388697
The worst part is that in my meeting with my boss today he didnt mention if I will pass the PIP. He said he needed to talk with some people including HR. So 90 percent chance he is going to fire me but just enough chance he wont that I have to spend the weekend waiting instead of processing the pain. And if he doesnt fire me, I will be a totally buckbroken employee who will never care about a rasie and work 60 hours weeks. Like a absurd housewife that has no were else to go and she tries to esapce and gets rapped(applies to ghost jobs) even worse so she comes back to him. Thats me. I used to think my relationship with work was like between a man and a woman, were I am the man that picks and chooses women and is the one who leaves but its all turned around past couple of years. I am nothing but a side pieace female whore to these companies now, they treat me like a contract worker without paying me like one. They got us with not giving raises to match inflation, they got us with return to work mandates and now, they are going to get us by firing us and leaving us with nothing. U will get put on a PIP too, at the worst time possible also. They wont even lay u off anymore, they want to save a little bit of money by denying us unemployment insurance by firing us through a PIP.


Was being in ur 30s a good excperince back in the day? There is no way human civilization would have gotten to were it is if it was always this bad for everyone. Of course, I am a faggot and just suck but the fsct of the matter is that PIPs and no jobs has been a very common trope on the internet lately

Banks, insurance, public sector, F500 companies, etc. They need software engineers and have no concept of PIPs. You'll get paid less and have less growth but better than being a blue collar wagie.

> EXTREMELY few (real) jobs available, as you mentioned
> whatever job position there is, gets insta-botted within minutes of posting by literally hundreds (and sometimes thousands) of resumes, so your chances are already shot if you're going through the mass apply route
So yeah, hate to be cliche but it's all about connections now. Caveat - the RIGHT connections, getting just connections might get you an interview but you're still very likely getting rejected again.

If a job is treating you like shit you have to move on. or get your manager fired. The latter is not easy.

If you own a small business you can also contribute about $44k to a 401k, plus $14k to your IRA, every year tax free and it doesn't count towards your $81k income. The easy credits are worth about $23k a year so your actual income is $104k plus an additional $58k for retirement savings. Total tax burden on all that is around $6k; less if you are ballsy with distributions vs payroll.
Pay off house and cars then coast on minimum work, very little tax and get a bunch of benefits.

If you’ve held Chainlink for years, ask yourself what you’ve been waiting for.

Open thread • Replies: 49 • Updated: 2026-06-20 06:39 UTC

OP

If you’ve held Chainlink for years, ask yourself what you’ve been waiting for.

LINK is a $5.7B token on $57.5M of annual fees. That’s ~99x revenue, and it has been flat for years, straight through every “banks are coming” cycle. The promise was always that adoption converts into value. It hasn’t. It’s been “soon” since 2020.

So here’s the question no diehard wants to answer:

What fee number, by what date, would make you sell? If LINK still earns ~$57M in 2028, is the thesis wrong, or is it still “soon”? If you can’t name the number and the date, you’re not investing in Chainlink. You’re believing in it. And belief has no exit.
